title: Create a GIF for your GitHub README
text: who_am_i_gif() requires three packages installed ghreadme default: camcorder records ggplot frame temporary PNG, gifski stitches PNGs final GIF. ggplot2 renders text plot frames.
code: install.packages(c("camcorder", "gifski", "ggplot2"))

title: Create a GIF for your GitHub README
text: Call who_am_i_gif() information pass who_am_i(). output argument controls GIF saved — set path inside profile repository: function prints message cumulative frame dark background, mimicking console output. last frame 4-second hold GIF loops, giving readers time read final line.
code: library(ghreadme)  who_am_i_gif(   name   = "Martin",   likes  = "#rstats and data visualization.",   learn  = "Shiny app development, Python, and Linux",   work   = "R package development tools.",   collab = "#rstats packages for data science.",   output = "intro.gif" )

title: Create a GIF for your GitHub README
code: who_am_i_gif(   name           = "Martin",   likes          = "#rstats and data visualization.",   learn          = "Shiny app development, Python, and Linux",   work           = "R package development tools.",   collab         = "#rstats packages for data science.",   output         = "intro.gif",   frame_duration = 2,   width          = 900,   height         = 450 )

title: Create a GIF for your GitHub README
text: Set connect = FALSE drop final “Want connect?” line:
code: who_am_i_gif(   name    = "Martin",   likes   = "#rstats and data visualization.",   learn   = "Shiny app development, Python, and Linux",   work    = "R package development tools.",   collab  = "#rstats packages for data science.",   connect = FALSE,   output  = "intro.gif" )

what: Embed the GIF in your README
title: Create a GIF for your GitHub README
text: profile repository, reference GIF top README.Rmd (badge links) plays visitors land profile: Knit README.Rmd regenerate README.md, commit files: profile page display animated introduction automatically.
code: --- title: "Hi there 👋" output: github_document ---    ![](intro.gif) git add README.Rmd README.md intro.gif git commit -m "update profile README" git push

title: Create a GIF for your GitHub README
code: library(ghreadme)  # 1. Generate the GIF into your profile repo who_am_i_gif(   name   = "Martin",   likes   = "#rstats and data visualization.",   learn   = "Shiny app development, Python, and Linux",   work    = "R package development tools.",   collab = "#rstats packages for data science.",   output = "path/to/mjfrigaard/intro.gif" )  # 2. Generate badge markdown for the README body gh_badges(   username = "mjfrigaard",   badge    = c("details", "commit_lang", "stats"),   theme    = "dark" )  # 3. Generate Stack Overflow badge HTML so_rep(username = "martin-frigaard", user_id = "4926446")

title: Animating Git visualizations with camcorder
text: recipes also assume data-collection ggplot-based plot helpers ghreadme:
code: install.packages(c("camcorder", "gifski")) library(ghreadme) library(camcorder)  commits <- collect_git_commits(   user   = "mjfrigaard",   emails = c("mjfrigaard@pm.me", "mjfrigaard@gmail.com") )

title: Animating Git visualizations with camcorder
text: camcorder::gg_record() registers hook every ggplot print() also written PNG inside temporary directory. loop finishes, camcorder::gg_playback() reads PNGs back order writes single GIF. pattern always: Call gg_record() start recording. Loop, building one ggplot per frame printing . Call gg_playback() render GIF. three recipes reuse skeleton differ plotted frame.
code: gg_record(   dir    = file.path(tempdir(), "viz-frames"),   device = "png",   width  = 1000,   height = 600,   units  = "px",   dpi    = 96,   bg     = "white" )  # ... loop printing one ggplot per frame ...  gg_playback(   name                 = "viz-git.gif",   first_image_duration = 1,   last_image_duration  = 4,   frame_duration       = 0.5,   image_resize         = 1000 )

what: Recipe 1: cumulative commits, sliding the end date
title: Animating Git visualizations with camcorder
text: cumulative_line_plot() already shows growth time, stepping date_end forward one month time makes growth feel kinetic — lines appear, lengthen, overtake one another repos accumulate commits.
code: date_seq <- seq(   from = as.Date("2023-01-01"),   to   = max(commits$date),   by   = "1 month" )  gg_record(   dir    = file.path(tempdir(), "cumulative-frames"),   device = "png",   width  = 1000,   height = 600,   units  = "px",   dpi    = 96,   bg     = "white" )  for (d in date_seq) {   p <- cumulative_line_plot(     commits,     date_begin = "2023-01-01",     date_end   = as.Date(d, origin = "1970-01-01"),     top_n      = 8   )   print(p) }  gg_playback(   name                 = "cumulative.gif",   first_image_duration = 1,   last_image_duration  = 4,   frame_duration       = 0.4,   image_resize         = 1000 )

what: Recipe 2: calendar heatmap, year by year
title: Animating Git visualizations with camcorder
text: calendar_heatmap_plot() strong fit slow reveal: frame adds one year history, GIF builds toward present. plot’s date_begin argument honoured filtering, need bump date_end end next year.
code: years <- sort(unique(commits$year))  gg_record(   dir    = file.path(tempdir(), "calendar-frames"),   device = "png",   width  = 1100,   height = 700,   units  = "px",   dpi    = 96,   bg     = "white" )  for (yr in years) {   p <- calendar_heatmap_plot(     commits,     date_begin = paste0(min(years), "-01-01"),     date_end   = paste0(yr, "-12-31"),     title      = paste("Through", yr)   )   print(p) }  gg_playback(   name                 = "calendar.gif",   first_image_duration = 1,   last_image_duration  = 4,   frame_duration       = 1.2,   image_resize         = 1100 )

title: Animating Git visualizations with camcorder
text: third pattern cycles repositories instead time — punchcard_plot() rendered per repo, GIF feels like flipping deck cards.
code: top_repos <- commits |>   dplyr::count(repo, sort = TRUE) |>   dplyr::slice_head(n = 6) |>   dplyr::pull(repo)  gg_record(   dir    = file.path(tempdir(), "punchcard-frames"),   device = "png",   width  = 1000,   height = 500,   units  = "px",   dpi    = 96,   bg     = "white" )  for (r in top_repos) {   p <- punchcard_plot(commits, repo = r, title = r)   print(p) }  gg_playback(   name                 = "punchcard.gif",   first_image_duration = 1,   last_image_duration  = 4,   frame_duration       = 1.5,   image_resize         = 1000 )

title: Animating Git visualizations with camcorder
text: Frame count vs file size. GIFs uncompressed across frames, monthly cadence five years (60 frames) renders nicely; daily cadence produces multi-megabyte file. Pick step yields 20–80 frames. Final-frame hold. last_image_duration (seconds) gives readers time absorb end state GIF loops. 3–5 seconds good range. Consistent axes. plots auto-scale per frame, makes axes jitter data added. bothers , pass explicit date_begin /date_end x-axis stays fixed across frames. Spiral plots ggplots. spiral_points_plot(), spiral_heatmap_plot(), spiral_horizon_plot() draw grid /spiralize graphics system, camcorder::gg_record() won’t capture . animate , render frame numbered PNG grDevices::png() / dev.() call gifski::gifski() directly file list.

title: Animating Git visualizations with camcorder
text: Drop rendered GIF profile README.Rmd way image — typically right badge block plays soon someone loads profile: Re-knit README.Rmd refresh README.md, commit GIF alongside .

title: Create a GitHub profile README
text: goal ghreadme create GitHub profile README.md using R Markdown.

title: Create a GitHub profile README
text: can install development version ghreadme like :
code: remotes::install_github("mjfrigaard/ghreadme")

title: Create a GitHub profile README
text: Load package, create repo name username (.e., https://github.com/<username>/<username>), add following README.Rmd file (set echo=FALSE hide code).
code: library(ghreadme) who_am_i(name = "Martin",   likes = "#rstats and data visualization.",   learn = "Shiny app development, Python, and Linux",   work = "R package development tools.",   collab = "#rstats packages for data science."   ) 👋 Hi, my name is Martin.       👀 I like #rstats and data visualization.       🌱 I'm learning about Shiny app development, Python, and Linux       📦 I'm currently working on R package development tools.       💞 I'd love to collaborate on #rstats packages for data science.       📫 Want to connect? Use the badges below...

title: Create a GitHub profile README
text: GitHub badges vercel.app, include call code chunk results set asis. Example:
code: ```{r gh_badges, results='asis'} gh_badges(username = "mjfrigaard",    badge = c("details", "stats", "repo_lang"),    theme = "dark") ```

title: Create a GitHub profile README
text: Example:
code: ```{r so_rep, results='asis'} so_rep(   username = "martin-frigaard",    user_id = "4926446") ```

title: GitHub-style calendar heatmap of daily commit counts — calendar_heatmap_plot
text: commits Tidy data frame collect_git_commits(). repo Optional repo name (vector). NULL aggregates repos. date_begin, date_end Optional Date bounds. title Plot title. Defaults repo name(s) "repos". week_start "Sunday" (GitHub default) "Monday" (ISO). low, high Hex colors fill gradient endpoints.
